risks.title: Tariff and trade policy on Chinese EVs and components
risks.narrative: Canada has aligned with the U.S. on a 100% surtax on Chinese-made EVs and additional duties on steel/aluminum. Vehicle-led entry is structurally exposed; infrastructure, software, and BESS components face a more navigable but still scrutinized path.
mitigations: Lead with charging hardware, BESS, software, and O&M rather than vehicles.
Source non-controversial components and consider Canadian/Mexican assembly for hardware.
Maintain optionality: design supply chain so vehicle entry can be deferred or skipped.

risks.title: Geopolitical sentiment and procurement scrutiny
risks.narrative: Public sector and critical-infrastructure procurement increasingly screen Chinese-origin technology. Brand and ownership structure must be unambiguous.
mitigations: Operate through a Canadian-incorporated entity with local board representation.
Use Canadian partners as the customer-facing brand where appropriate.
Pre-engage federal/provincial procurement and security review processes.

risks.title: Grid interconnection delays
risks.narrative: Utility queue times of 9–18 months are common for DCFC and fleet depot loads, especially in Ontario and BC.
mitigations: Pair sites with BESS to defer or shrink interconnect upgrades.
Pre-screen sites for available capacity before signing leases.
Sign master cooperation agreements with utilities early.

risks.title: Permitting and municipal coordination
risks.narrative: Charging deployment touches municipal zoning, fire code, and electrical inspection. Timelines vary widely by jurisdiction.
mitigations: Standardize a permit-ready site design package per province.
Engage municipal economic development offices early.
Prioritize provinces and cities with streamlined EV permitting.

risks.title: Public charging utilization below pro-forma
risks.narrative: Highway and urban DCFC sites can take 18–30 months to mature. Underutilization is the dominant downside in scenario modeling.
mitigations: Anchor early sites with fleet contracts (depot + en-route).
Diversify revenue with BESS market participation and software fees.
Use cost-share funding to keep payback within tolerable range even at low utilization.

risks.title: FX and supply chain volatility
risks.narrative: CAD/USD/CNY swings and global cell supply tightness can move project capex by ±15%.
mitigations: Hedge major equipment orders.
Qualify two suppliers per critical component.
Stage capex commitments in tranches tied to phase gates.

risks.title: Cybersecurity and data residency
risks.narrative: EV chargers and BESS are connected critical infrastructure. Canadian customers will demand data residency and supply-chain security attestations.
mitigations: Host all customer telemetry in Canadian cloud regions.
Adopt OCPP 2.0.1 with security profile 3 and ISO 15118 plug-and-charge.
Independent third-party penetration testing before commercial launch.

risks.title: Local talent and labor
risks.narrative: Certified electricians and station commissioning engineers are constrained.
mitigations: Long-term framework agreements with regional electrical contractors.
In-house training program for commissioning and O&M.
Modular site design that reduces on-site labor hours.
